content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660              322-5901              322-6441              222-6444

October 21, 1980


James D. Boitano
Napa County District
Attorney
P.O. Box 148
Napa, CA  94558

Dear Mr. Boitano:

     This is in response to your letter of October 18, 1980.
You have asked whether it was proper for Napa County to have
included you, as District Attorney, as a designated employee
in its Conflict of Interest Code. The following advice is
provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b).

     Government Code Section 87302 provides what the contents
of a Conflict of Interest Code adopted pursuant to the Political
Reform Act should be. Section 87302(b), which covers the
disclosure provisions of a Code, provides that:

     . . . The provisions of the Conflict of Interest Code
     adopted under this subsection shall not be applicable
     to any designated employee who is covered by Article 2
     of this chapter.

                              [Emphasis added.]

     Therefore, although it is proper for Napa County to
include you in its Conflict of Interest Code for disquali-
fication purposes, provided for in Government Code Section
87302(c), you should not be required to file a disclosure
statement under the Code. Because the position of district
attorney is one of the positions listed in Government Code
Section 87200, you are instead required to file a Form 721
pursuant to Article 2 of Chapter 7 of the Political Reform
Act. This is the only disclosure obligation the Act imposes
upon you.

                                                            87302(c)

Mr. James D. Boitano
October 21, 1980
Page Two


     Beginning January 1, 1981, the Political Reform Act
will provide a broad range of remedies for a violation of
its disqualification requirement, contained in Government
Code Section 87100. Until then, however, although the full
range of remedies was available for the violation of a dis-
qualification provision of a Conflict of Interest Code, the
only remedy for a violation of the disqualification provision
of the Act itself was overturning the decision. See Government Code Section 87102. Consequently, Article 2 filers had
to be designated in Conflict of Interest Codes for disquali-
fication purposes, although they expressly should not be
designated for disclosure purposes. Be assured that Napa
County was not the only local government that failed to see
the distinction between these two kinds of designation. The
remedies for violation of Section 87100 of the Act which
will be available starting January 1, 1981, will eliminate
the need for Article 2 filers to be designated in Codes at
all, and this will hopefully end some of the confusion which
led to your current situation.

     If you have any further questions, please feel free to
call me, or to refer the County Administrator or the County
Counsel to me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Sarah T. Cameron
                                        Deputy Chief for
                                        Conflicts of Interest
                                        Legal Division

STC:nw

NAPA COUNTY                 DISTRICT ATTORNEY
PUBLIC ADMINISTRATOR — PUBLIC GUARDIAN

JAMES D. BOITANO                                    HALL OF JUSTICE • 1125 THIRD STREET • P. O. BOX 148
District Attorney                                  NAPA, CALIFORNIA 94558         707-253-4211


                                                   October 16, 1980




Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California, P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA          95804

                    RE:  Napa County Conflict of Interest Code

Dear Ladies and Gentlemen:

As you know, Napa County has enacted a Conflict of Interest Code.
However, over my strong objection, they have included the District
Attorney as one of the individuals that must file form 730-State-
ment of Economic Interest for designated employees.

Again, as you know, I am mandated by State law to file an almost
exact duplicate of such a statement and my question to you is
whether this is not an improper duplication of effort.

In other words, since State law has preempted this particular area,
is County legislation not improper?

As a practical matter, this is another example of the bureaucratic
duplication which is so costly in both time and money.


                                                   Very truly yours,


                                                   [signature]
                                                   JAMES D. BOITANO
                                                   District Attorney

JDB:clb

cc:  Mr. Albert J. Haberger
     County Administrator

     Mr. Stephen Hackett
     County Counsel

sections.question: Since State law has mandated that the District Attorney file an almost exact duplicate statement (Form 730-Statement of Economic Interest) and Napa County has included the District Attorney as one of the individuals that must file the same form under their Conflict of Interest Code, is this not an improper duplication of effort? Since State law has preempted this particular area, is County legislation not improper?
sections.question_synthetic: The District Attorney of Napa County questions whether requiring him to file duplicate Economic Interest statements under both state law and the county's Conflict of Interest Code constitutes improper duplication, given that state law has preempted this regulatory area.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a District Attorney to the FPPC, not an FPPC response. The document quality is poor with significant OCR/scanning artifacts in the header section, but the main body is legible. No formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections are present, so question and conclusion text were synthesized from the letter's content. No FPPC response is included in this document.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Since State law has mandated that the District Attorney file an almost exact duplicate statement (Form 730-Statement of Economic Interest) and Napa County has included the District Attorney as one of the individuals that must file the same form under their Conflict of Interest Code, is this not an improper duplication of effort? Since State law has preempted this particular area, is County legislation not improper?

embedding.summary: District Attorney James D. Boitano writes to the FPPC to challenge Napa County's Conflict of Interest Code requirement that he file Form 730 under county law, arguing this duplicates the state-mandated filing requirement and represents improper county legislation that conflicts with state preemption.
